video_title: David Silber, Head of Institutional Equity Derivatives, Citadel Securities
gist: David Silber, head of institutional equity derivatives at Citadel Securities, argues listed options liquidity is now so deep it can replicate most OTC/structured objectives, walks through the explosion in volumes (from ~10-12M contracts/day a decade ago to ~80M/day in June 2025 and a record ~$8T notional expiration), and explains how Citadel is porting the frictionless, low-touch, zero-commission retail model into institutional options.

insights.claim: US listed options volume has roughly quadrupled in about a decade, from ~10-12 million contracts/day pre-COVID to ~55 million a year ago and on track for ~80 million contracts/day in June 2025.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Structural surge in options ADV signals durable demand for gamma/liquidity that market makers can monetize and that keeps spreads tight for end users.
insights.quote: about a year ago where options volumes were already you know at all-time highs we were trading about 55 million contracts a day and I think June of this year is on track to be just about 80 million contracts a day

insights.claim: The June 2025 options expiration was the largest in history at an estimated ~$8 trillion of notional, of which roughly 60% was in the S&P (SPX, not SPY) on the morning expiration.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Record index expiration concentration amplifies dealer hedging flows around monthly expiry and reinforces expiry as a key volatility/positioning inflection to watch.
insights.quote: estimates are very much around eight trillion dollars of option notional expiry this past Thursday, of which about 60ish% of it was in the S&P on that on that morning expiration

insights.claim: Single-stock options activity has grown so much that Micron's June expiration carried more notional than the IWM/Russell 2000, showing volume broadening well beyond the usual Tesla/Nvidia names.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Single-name option depth now rivals major index ETFs, opening liquid single-stock overlay and hedging trades that were previously impractical.
insights.quote: I think Micron expired you know more notional than the IWM or the Russell

insights.claim: The average institution on Citadel's desk trades options expiring in under 40 days, and the retail options user is roughly half that maturity or even shorter-dated.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: The persistent shift to ultra-short tenor concentrates dealer gamma near-the-money and near-expiry, a structural driver of intraday volatility dynamics.
insights.quote: the average institution is trading options that expire in less than 40 days on average on our desk

insights.claim: Zero-DTE options are not merely a retail phenomenon; macro hedge funds actively use them for stop-outs and delta-substitution purposes, with Citadel Securities frequently on the other side.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Sophisticated institutional zero-DTE usage means these flows carry positioning information, not just noise, and reflect deliberate delta management by large players.
insights.quote: macro hedge funds using zero DTE for again stopout purposes, things like that, delta substitution purposes

insights.claim: Institutions use zero-DTE options to accelerate exiting a position or to reduce delta exposure while working out of an equity position over time with less market impact, spending premium to buy discretion.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Explains a mechanism behind zero-DTE demand—impact reduction on large unwinds—useful for interpreting index/sector 0DTE flow as stealth position management.
insights.quote: they might be able to spend some premium on zero DTE to reduce their exposure to that delta while they're working out of it over a longer period of time

insights.claim: Listed options liquidity and the breadth of strikes/expiries are now deep enough that institutions can replicate objectives previously solved with OTC instruments or QIS products using the lit markets.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: A secular threat to bespoke OTC/QIS structuring revenue and a tailwind for exchange-traded liquidity providers as clients migrate to lit markets.
insights.quote: The reality is now listed options the liquidity is so deep. The expirations and strikes are are so robust that you can replicate a lot of your objectives by using the lit markets

insights.claim: The key advantage of listed over exotic OTC derivatives is a transparent 'return policy'—a known, tight bid/offer to unwind—whereas changing your mind on a light-exotic OTC trade means getting 'whacked pretty good.'
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Frames exit liquidity/unwind cost as a decisive selection criterion favoring listed products, a lens for evaluating any derivatives structure.
insights.quote: when you buy something, you should know the return policy... I've done plenty in in, you know, light exotic OTC derivatives... but the return policy isn't great. You change your mind, you're going to get, you know, whacked pretty good

insights.claim: Micron exemplifies a self-reinforcing spot-up vol-up regime, up roughly 10-fold in a year with implied vol up ~100, echoing the NASDAQ/Qs behavior into the 2000 peak rather than the low-vol instabilities of the pre-GFC period.
insights.speaker: Dean Curnutt
insights.so_what: Instability now clusters in winning high-vol momentum names rather than low-vol complacency, a different risk map for tail hedging and skew positioning.
insights.quote: the thing is up 10fold in a year V is up 100 or something... In some ways the instabilities are very much associated with highvall and they're winning stock

insights.claim: In spot-up vol-up single names, realized volatility can outpace implied even after events, a new market regime that complicates dealer risk management around large-cap momentum stocks.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Post-event realized-over-implied dynamics invert the usual vol-crush trade, warning against reflexively selling event vol in momentum names.
insights.quote: you do have a spot up V-up regime in some cases you realize V even outpacing implied V post events which are you know new market regimes

insights.claim: Dealers face offsetting client flows in momentum names: income/overwriting funds sell ~25-delta calls for yield while retail investors buy ~30-delta calls chasing momentum after strong earnings.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific supply (yield sellers) and demand (retail momentum buyers) that shape upside call skew and give market makers two-sided flow to intermediate.
insights.quote: income funds collecting yield by taking advantage of that by selling 25 delta calls... retail investors, you know, gaining momentum and looking for access to the product on the back of a strong earnings day, buying 30 delta calls

insights.claim: Silber's 2019 Citadel business plan was built on porting to institutional options the same disruption that already hit cash equities (algos/central risk book cutting headcount and cost) and retail options (zero commission, instant fills, better prices).
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: The competitive playbook—remove friction, pass technology edge to clients—predicts further electronification and margin compression in institutional options intermediation.
insights.quote: if we can create that experience for an institutional customer and client in options massive differentiator the customer will demand this

insights.claim: The cash equities institutional business became algorithmic around 2010, shrinking headcount and print sizes while cutting trade cost and market impact and ultimately improving investor outcomes.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: A template for how options intermediation evolves—fewer traders, more quants/technologists, tighter pricing—informing where headcount and margins migrate next.
insights.quote: somewhere around 2010 call that business became more algorithmic. It was the rise of the central risk book, the rise of algos and what you really saw happening was headcount began to go down

insights.claim: Citadel Securities launched Silber's institutional derivatives effort around Labor Day 2019, building a ~20-person team that he claims is now one of the leading listed options houses in the institutional space.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Signals Citadel's rapid encroachment on bank-dominated institutional derivatives franchises, pressuring incumbent sell-side desks.
insights.quote: I started I think two weeks later in Labor Day of 2019 and shortly after Jason Roki joined to run sales and since then we've we've built a team of about 20 people all in

insights.claim: Citadel Securities offers institutional options clients a low-touch electronic execution channel—mirroring cash equities—for both zero-dated and longer-dated options, not just voice block trades.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Electronic institutional options execution is a differentiator that captures flow migrating away from voice-intermediated trading.
insights.quote: We have an electronic offering that actually allows institutional clients that same lowtouch experience which dominated the cash equities world

insights.claim: Modern derivatives risk management is less about warehousing and delta-hedging one position and more about recycling risk across many channels—on-screen, retail, other products, other regions, and the institutional channel.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Scale and multi-channel access are now the moat in liquidity provision; smaller players without those channels can't compete on block risk transfer.
insights.quote: It used to be if you sold one call, you went out and bought some stock and maybe you tried to buy another call. Those days have changed just based on the sheer size of the market

insights.claim: With events now happening constantly, being a specialist in specific catalysts (PDUFA dates, Apple product cycles) matters less than making risk transfer seamless via deep aggregate liquidity.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Reframes edge in options market-making from event expertise toward liquidity aggregation and execution efficiency.
insights.quote: the day of having to be a expert in the next, you know, financial event or PDUFA date in healthcare or, you know, Mac day in Apple... Nowadays, the reality is there's events all the time

insights.claim: The 1999 arrival of multi-listing at the CBOE ended single-venue option franchises (e.g., Dell only in Philadelphia, Coca-Cola only in Chicago), driving traders to physically crowd Chicago pits to capture rerouted order flow.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Historical marker for how competition and venue fragmentation began reshaping options liquidity, context for today's 18-venue electronic landscape.
insights.quote: it was the the coming of multilisting. So the days of trading a a Dell option only in Philadelphia or a Coca-Cola option only in Chicago, those days were coming to an end

insights.claim: Wide option skew historically was partly a microstructure artifact: fractional pricing (eighths/16ths until ~2001 nickels/dimes, penny pilot only ~2007-08) limited price points and price discovery.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Cautions against comparing historical skew levels to today's—tick-size regime, not just risk appetite, drove the numbers.
insights.quote: things traded in, you know, eighths or 16s and that created just by nature less price discovery

insights.claim: Paul Allen's multi-billion-dollar Microsoft collar around 2001 left a massive skew footprint—Silber recalls the Oct 55/90 collar with about 13 vols of skew between the 55 put and 90 call—and even a name like Microsoft hit a hard limit where dealers refused more risk.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Illustrates how a single large hedge can dominate a single-name skew and how thin floor-era capacity was versus today's deep liquidity.
insights.quote: There was like 13 vols of skew between the 55 put and the 90 call

insights.claim: Citadel Securities monetizes anonymized, delayed retail options flow data as a client-facing edge, publishing pieces like 'the retail detail' (with Scott Rubner's team) because retail is now a meaningful, not marginal, market participant.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Positioning data on retail flow is a differentiated research product institutions use to gauge their own setup relative to the crowd.
insights.quote: I think Scott Rubner from our team has written quite a bit about they are not, you know, a market participant. They are a meaningful part of the market at this point

insights.claim: Sell-side derivatives research has commoditized—vendors and cheap tech now solve the old implied-vol-percentile spreadsheets—so edge shifts to bespoke, task-force-style client solutions and thought-leadership rather than mass duplicative emails.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Signals where sell-side research value is migrating; generic vol dashboards no longer differentiate a desk.
insights.quote: in the age of technology and vendors and you know the the price of things getting so cheap, a lot of those things have been solved for clients uh without the need of a sellside platform

insights.claim: Silber argues Citadel's structural advantage is a genuine sales-trading incentive alignment around client experience and net P&L, avoiding the siloed sales-vs-trading bonus-pool competition endemic to large banks.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Aligned incentives are pitched as the reason a non-bank liquidity provider can out-serve incumbent bank desks and win institutional flow.
insights.quote: in many ways at a large organization people are competing with each other... for EV for trading P&L and quite frankly for bonus pool dollars

insights.claim: Citadel Securities pitches recruits and clients on a workflow value trade: paying a penny more per option to reclaim an hour a day of PM time, though it claims scale and automation actually let it charge a penny less.
insights.speaker: David Silber
insights.so_what: Reframes execution competition around total workflow/time cost rather than per-contract price, the argument used to win institutional mandates.
insights.quote: If I told somebody, you're going to pay a penny more for every single option, but you're going to get an hour extra a day. I think most institutional clients would be happy
